SB1086.title: Zoning: allowed uses: microschools and micro-education entities.
SB1086.author: Megan Dahle
SB1086.summary: An act to add Section 65852.10 to the Government Code, relating to land use.

actions.description: Introduced. Read first time. To Com. on RLS. for assignment. To print.
From printer. May be acted upon on or after March 16.

analysis.notes: SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10, which would require cities and counties to allow microschools and micro-education entities as permitted uses in zoning districts — overriding local land use decisions about where educational uses are appropriate. This constitutes a partial preemption of local zoning authority (Criterion A), likely mandates ministerial or by-right approval for qualifying micro-education uses (Criterion B/C), and shifts permitting and compliance administration costs to local governments without evident state funding (Criterion D).
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1086 adds Government Code §65852.10 to require cities and counties to permit microschools and micro-education entities as allowed uses in local zoning, overriding local land use authority to determine where educational uses are appropriate.
• Preempts local zoning authority by mandating that microschools be treated as allowed uses regardless of a city's or county's general plan and zoning code designations.
• Likely eliminates or severely restricts local discretionary review — conditional use permits, design review, and neighborhood compatibility hearings — for qualifying micro-education facilities.
• Forces cities to approve a new category of institutional use that communities have not chosen to permit, effectively mandating development decisions beyond local preference.
• Shifts administrative and compliance costs onto local planning departments without identified state funding to offset the new permitting and enforcement obligations.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill explicitly overrides local zoning authority across all district types with no local opt-out or mitigation mechanism, and strips discretionary review for a new institutional use category cities have not chosen to permit.

analysis.comms_brief:
SB1003 adds state procedural requirements to development approvals, limiting conditions cities may apply and shortening the time available for local deliberation.
• Limits local conditions: restricts legitimate protective conditions cities attach to housing approvals, reducing mitigation authority for project impacts
• Mandatory timelines: short state deadlines for local response reduce capacity for thorough technical review, especially in complex or contested projects
• Compressed appeals: abbreviated local appeal processes limit community challenge opportunities for residents affected by nearby development decisions
Recommended: CSF should oppose; engage city engineers and attorneys to document workability concerns and impacts on local capacity.

AB1751.title: Missing Middle Townhome Ownership Act.
AB1751.author: Sharon Quirk-Silva

analysis.notes: Creates a statewide ministerial pathway for townhomes on any residential parcel, directly preempting local zoning and eliminating all discretionary review; cities cannot require impact fees or infrastructure improvements for new townhome residents.
analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB1751 (Missing Middle Townhome Ownership Act) creates a statewide ministerial pathway for townhome development on any residential parcel, overriding local single-family zoning without community input.
• Cities lose all discretionary authority — projects meeting state objective standards must be approved regardless of neighborhood character, infrastructure capacity, or local planning
• Eliminates design review and public hearings for qualifying developments; no CEQA review applies
• No impact fee authority: cities cannot charge water, sewer, traffic, or school impact fees for new townhome residents — infrastructure costs shift entirely to existing taxpayers
• Sets a precedent for further ministerial preemption of residential zoning across the state
Recommended: CSF should actively oppose; submit formal comment letters and coordinate with planning directors to document infrastructure and service capacity concerns.

AB1997.title: Land use: housing development approvals: timelines and processes.
AB1997.author: Alex Lee
AB1997.summary: An act to amend Sections 65589.5, 65589.9, and 65950 of, and to add Section 65943.3 to, the Government Code, and to add Section 50411 to the Health and Safety Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 20.

analysis.notes: AB 1997 amends Housing Accountability Act provisions (§65589.5, §65589.9) and approval timeline statutes (§65950), and adds new streamlining mandates (§65943.3, HSC §50411). Taken together, these amendments tighten state control over local approval timelines, limit cities' ability to deny or condition housing projects under the HAA, and impose compliance requirements on local agencies — all of which restrict local discretion and shift infrastructure review authority away from cities without clear funding offsets.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1997 amends the Housing Accountability Act and local approval timeline statutes to restrict cities' ability to deny housing projects, compress permitting timelines, and impose new compliance mandates on local agencies.
• Amendments to §65589.5 (HAA) further limit the findings cities may use to deny or condition housing projects, narrowing local discretion over project approval.
• Changes to §65950 compress or restructure local approval timelines, reducing cities' capacity to conduct thorough infrastructure, design, and community review before project approval.
• Addition of §65943.3 and HSC §50411 creates new streamlining mandates and compliance obligations on local agencies with no clear state funding offset for staffing or administrative costs.
• Tightened HAA enforcement under §65589.9 increases litigation exposure for cities that attempt to apply objective local standards or deny projects on capacity grounds.
Recommended: Oppose — bill layers multiple HAA and timeline restrictions that cumulatively strip local discretion over project conditions, timelines, and denial authority with no mechanism to replace lost infrastructure review safeguards.

AB2005.title: Housing developments: urban lot split: parcel maps.
AB2005.author: Patrick Ahrens
AB2005.summary: An act to amend Section 66411.7 of the Government Code, relating to land use.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 20.

analysis.notes: AB 2005 amends Government Code §66411.7, which governs urban lot splits (SB 9's parcel map provisions). Amendments to this section typically expand or clarify ministerial by-right approval of urban lot splits, further constraining local discretion to deny or condition such subdivisions. The summary is too brief to confirm the precise mechanism, so full text review is warranted; however, any amendment to §66411.7 carries strong a priori risk of deepening state preemption of local subdivision and zoning authority, eliminating discretionary review for qualifying splits, and mandating approval of density increases cities have not locally chosen.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2005 amends the SB 9 urban lot split statute (Gov. Code §66411.7), likely expanding or clarifying ministerial by-right parcel approvals that override local zoning and subdivision authority.
• Amendments to §66411.7 have historically expanded the set of parcels eligible for ministerial lot splits, removing cities' ability to apply local subdivision standards or deny nonconforming splits.
• Ministerial approval under this section strips cities of discretionary review, public hearings, and design conditions for qualifying urban lot split applications.
• By mandating approval of lot splits that effectively double allowable density on single-family parcels, the bill forces cities to accommodate development volumes beyond locally adopted general plan and zoning choices.
• Infrastructure and utility capacity review is severely constrained under ministerial approval, potentially shifting connection and frontage costs to existing residents without city recourse.
Recommended: Oppose pending full text review — any expansion of §66411.7's ministerial approval scope directly and materially erodes city zoning, subdivision, and infrastructure conditioning authority with no local opt-out.

AB2118.title: Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022: use by right: objective standards.
AB2118.author: Josh Hoover
AB2118.summary: An act to amend Sections 65912.101 and 65912.123 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 21.

analysis.notes: This bill amends the Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022 (Gov. Code §§65912.101, 65912.123), which already establishes a use-by-right approval pathway. Amendments to these sections most likely expand or clarify the ministerial approval mandate and the role of objective standards — both of which directly preempt local discretionary zoning authority, eliminate CEQA and hearing rights for qualifying projects, and compel cities to approve qualifying affordable housing projects regardless of local land use preferences. The infrastructure burden is moderate given ministerial approval pathways that limit cities' ability to condition approvals on utility or frontage improvements.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2118 amends the Affordable Housing and High Road Jobs Act of 2022 to modify use-by-right approval rules and objective standards, further constraining how cities may review and condition qualifying affordable housing projects.
• Expands or entrenches ministerial (no-discretion) approval for qualifying projects, stripping cities of case-by-case zoning and land use authority under §65912.101.
• Limits cities to "objective standards" only under §65912.123, eliminating design review, conditional use permits, and public hearings that communities rely on for neighborhood-sensitive development decisions.
• Forces cities to approve qualifying projects regardless of local general plan or zoning preferences, effectively mandating development volumes and types beyond community choice.
• Ministerial approval pathways constrain cities' ability to require infrastructure improvements or utility upgrades as conditions of approval, shifting potential capacity costs to existing residents.
Recommended: Oppose — bill entrenches state preemption of local planning authority with no meaningful mechanism for communities to ensure projects are compatible with local infrastructure capacity or neighborhood character.

AB2127.summary: An act relating to land use.

AB2288.title: Adaptive reuse: industrial zones.
AB2288.author: Sharon Quirk-Silva
AB2288.summary: An act to amend Sections 65658.1 and 65658.5 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 22.

analysis.notes: Forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial zones, preempting employment land protections; industrial zones lack residential utility infrastructure and cities cannot require developers to fund water, sewer, pedestrian, or parks upgrades needed for residential occupancy.
analysis.model: claude-sonnet-4-6 (via Claude Code) — D criterion reanalysis
analysis.comms_brief:
AB2288 forces cities to allow residential conversions in industrial-zoned areas, directly preempting employment land protections that communities have established through local planning.
• Preempts industrial land use designations: cities lose authority over employment zone preservation, undermining economic development strategies and tax base
• Threatens jobs and economic development: converting industrial to residential removes land cities rely on for tax-generating employment — a one-way transition with no reversal
• Infrastructure mismatch: industrial zones lack residential utilities, parks, and pedestrian infrastructure — cities cannot require developers to fund the needed upgrades for livable residential use
• No local environmental review: conversions proceed ministerially even on sites with contamination history or incompatible adjacent operations
Recommended: CSF should strongly oppose; partner with economic development organizations and labor groups on employment land preservation advocacy.

AB2295.title: Regional housing need: affordable housing.
AB2295.author: Natasha Johnson
AB2295.summary: An act to add Section 65584.2.3 to the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 22.

analysis.notes: AB 2295 adds Section 65584.2.3 to the Government Code, which relates to the Regional Housing Need Allocation (RHNA) process with a focus on affordable housing. Bills modifying RHNA methodology or allocation formulas partially constrain how cities plan and zone by adjusting the state-imposed housing quotas they must accommodate, representing a moderate local control override and moderate mandate pressure. The bill's early stage and limited digest prevent precise scoring of infrastructure cost impacts, though RHNA-driven compliance obligations routinely generate indirect staffing and planning burdens for localities.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2295 modifies the Regional Housing Need Allocation process to adjust how affordable housing need is calculated and assigned to cities and counties, potentially increasing or reshaping local housing quotas outside of local deliberation.
• RHNA changes constrain local General Plan and zoning choices by setting state-imposed numerical targets cities must plan for, regardless of local infrastructure, capacity, or community preference.
• Revised allocation formulas may increase the share of affordable units cities are required to site, effectively directing land use policy from Sacramento rather than city hall.
• Cities that fail to meet adjusted RHNA targets face builder's remedy exposure and loss of local zoning authority, creating strong compliance pressure even if penalties are not explicit in this bill.
Recommended: Monitor — bill is pre-committee with limited text available; risk level depends heavily on whether the allocation formula increases local quotas or imposes new methodology; full bill text should be reviewed once available.

analysis.comms_brief:

• Amendments to §65912.157 typically expand or extend ministerial/by-right approval pathways, stripping cities of project-level discretion over qualifying TOD housing proposals.
• Existing TOD streamlining law limits the objective standards and conditions cities may impose, constraining local design review and conditional use permit authority.
• Any expansion of project eligibility or approval mandates forces cities to approve density or housing types beyond what local planning processes would otherwise allow.
• Increased development density near transit stations creates indirect infrastructure capacity pressure without confirmed local mitigation authority.
Recommended: Monitor — bill is newly introduced with no committee analysis or amendments available; full text review needed to confirm scope of changes to ministerial approval thresholds and eligibility criteria.

AB2390.title: Streamlined housing approvals: hazardous waste sites.
AB2390.author: Pilar Schiavo
AB2390.summary: An act to amend Section 65913.4 of the Government Code, relating to housing.

actions.description: Read first time. To print.
From printer. May be heard in committee March 23.

analysis.notes: AB 2390 amends Government Code §65913.4 — the SB 35 streamlined ministerial approval statute — to extend its by-right, no-discretion approval pathway to housing projects on hazardous waste sites. This is a strong local control override because SB 35 already eliminates CEQA review and discretionary hearings for qualifying projects; expanding it to contaminated sites further strips cities of authority to condition or deny projects based on site-specific environmental and safety concerns. The infrastructure/capacity burden is moderate: ministerial approval on hazardous sites may prevent cities from imposing remediation-linked conditions or delaying approvals pending cleanup verification.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2390 expands California's SB 35 ministerial (by-right) approval statute to housing projects on hazardous waste sites, stripping cities of discretionary review authority over contaminated properties.
• Eliminates local discretionary review and CEQA applicability for qualifying projects on hazardous waste sites, removing the primary tool cities use to evaluate remediation adequacy before residents move in.
• Extends state preemption of local zoning and development standards to a new category of environmentally sensitive sites, where community-specific conditions are most critical.
• Prevents cities from conditioning or delaying approvals pending independent verification of site cleanup, shifting liability and safety risk onto future residents and neighboring communities.
• Limits ability of cities to impose site-specific infrastructure or remediation-related conditions that would otherwise be required through discretionary review.
Recommended: Oppose — ministerial approval on hazardous waste sites removes the last local checkpoint for public health and safety review, with no replacement mechanism for cities to ensure adequate remediation before occupancy.

Read second time and amended.

analysis.notes: AB 2433 amends Government Code §65915 (the Density Bonus Law), which by design preempts local zoning by compelling cities to grant density increases, reduced parking, and other concessions to qualifying projects beyond what local plans would otherwise allow. Amendments to this statute typically expand the universe of eligible projects, increase bonus percentages, or broaden the menu of required concessions/incentives — each of which further limits the conditions cities may attach to approvals and reduces discretionary review scope. Infrastructure burden is indirect: more density bonus projects mean greater permitting load and potential capacity strain, but the statute does not explicitly eliminate impact fee authority.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2433 amends California's Density Bonus Law (Gov. Code §65915), expanding state mandates that require cities to grant density increases and development concessions beyond what local zoning would permit.
• Local Control Override: Density Bonus Law amendments directly preempt local zoning by compelling cities to approve additional units and concessions regardless of community land use plans.
• Removes Discretionary Review: Qualifying density bonus projects already receive ministerial-track processing; amendments that expand eligibility or concession requirements further reduce the conditions and design standards cities may lawfully impose.
• Mandates Development: Any expansion of bonus percentages, eligible project types, or required incentives constitutes an effective state mandate that cities approve greater density than local deliberation would produce.
Recommended: Monitor pending amended text — if amendments broaden eligibility thresholds or increase mandatory concession counts, escalate to Oppose on grounds of diminished local planning authority and no offsetting infrastructure funding mechanism.

analysis.notes: AB 2480 expands California's Density Bonus Law (Gov. Code §65915) to enhance density bonus entitlements for student housing developments, which by statute compels cities to grant additional density beyond what local zoning would otherwise allow — a conditional preemption of local zoning authority. Density bonus law is ministerially applied once eligibility criteria are met, limiting discretionary review and the conditions cities may impose on qualifying projects.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2480 amends the state Density Bonus Law to extend or enhance bonus density entitlements for student housing developments, compelling cities to approve greater density than local zoning permits for qualifying projects.
• Overrides local zoning authority by mandating density bonuses cities must grant regardless of local general plan or zoning designations.
• Density bonus approvals are ministerially applied, stripping cities of discretionary review, design review flexibility, and ability to condition projects on community compatibility.
• Cities cannot decline to grant the bonus once eligibility is established, effectively mandating higher-density student housing development beyond local choice.
• Increased density at ministerial approval may pressure existing infrastructure (water, sewer, parking) without adequate local authority to require developer-funded mitigation.
Recommended: Monitor — bill text is not yet in print; risk level depends on the specific bonus percentages and eligibility thresholds added. Oppose if amendments materially expand mandatory bonus tiers or further restrict local conditions on student housing projects.

analysis.notes: SB 1353 amends Health & Safety Code §11834.23, which governs how cities and counties may treat alcohol/drug recovery and treatment facilities in residential zones. Existing law generally requires these facilities (6 or fewer residents) to be treated as a residential use by right — this bill likely modifies the conditions or scope of that mandate, potentially expanding the categories of facilities entitled to residential use classification or limiting the local conditions cities may impose. The local control risk stems from the state overriding local zoning discretion to compel residential treatment of these facilities and limiting what local governments may require before granting that status.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 1353 amends state law governing when alcohol and drug recovery or treatment facilities must be treated as a residential use of property, potentially expanding state preemption of local zoning and limiting local conditions on these facilities.
• Existing §11834.23 already requires cities to classify qualifying facilities as residential by right — amendments could broaden the facilities entitled to this override, reducing local discretion over what facilities operate in residential neighborhoods.
• By mandating residential use classification, the bill limits cities' ability to apply local zoning standards, neighborhood compatibility review, or conditions tailored to community context.
• Any expansion of "by right" residential status further erodes the ability of local governments to evaluate siting, density of similar uses, or proximity concerns through discretionary review.
Recommended: Monitor — the full bill text is not available and the specific amendment language is critical to determining whether this materially expands state preemption beyond current law; fetch full text before taking a formal position.

analysis.notes: AB 748 requires cities and counties to establish a preapproved plan program for single-family and multifamily housing, compelling local governments to accept state-blessed prototype plans and process them ministerially — limiting local design review and objective standard application. This partially overrides local land use authority by mandating a new approval pathway, compresses discretionary review for qualifying projects, and imposes unfunded administrative burdens on local planning departments to develop, maintain, and administer the preapproval program.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 748 mandates that cities and counties establish and administer a preapproved housing plan program, creating a streamlined ministerial-style approval pathway that bypasses normal local discretionary review for qualifying single-family and multifamily projects.
• Partial preemption of local authority: cities must accept and process preapproved prototype plans, limiting their ability to apply locally-adopted design standards or require project-specific review.
• Removes discretionary review: qualifying projects submitted under preapproved plans are processed outside standard public hearing, design review, and conditional use permit processes.
• Mandates development approval: cities are effectively required to approve projects conforming to preapproved plans, reducing community choice over housing type and design in local neighborhoods.
• Unfunded compliance burden: local planning departments must build and maintain a new preapproval administrative infrastructure with no guaranteed state funding offset.
Recommended: Oppose — the bill imposes a mandatory preapproval program that erodes local design authority and generates unfunded administrative costs with no mechanism to recover expenses or preserve meaningful community input.

analysis.notes: SB 677 amends Government Code §65912.156, which sits within California's existing Transit-Oriented Development (TOD) streamlining framework. TOD statutes in this section typically mandate ministerial, by-right approval for qualifying housing projects near transit, overriding local zoning and eliminating discretionary review including CEQA. The amendment likely expands eligibility criteria, removes existing local conditions, or strengthens the ministerial approval mandate — all of which are strong local control overrides. Infrastructure burden is moderate because ministerial approvals under TOD law typically restrict the infrastructure conditions and objective standards cities may impose.
analysis.comms_brief:
SB 677 amends California's transit-oriented development streamlining statute (Gov. Code §65912.156) to expand or strengthen mandatory ministerial approval of housing projects near transit, overriding local zoning, eliminating discretionary review, and limiting cities' ability to impose conditions.
• Explicit state preemption of local zoning: qualifying TOD projects must be approved ministerially regardless of what local plans or community standards allow
• Elimination of CEQA and discretionary review: cities lose the ability to conduct environmental review, hold public hearings, or apply design review to covered projects
• Mandatory development approval: cities are compelled to approve projects that meet state-defined criteria, removing local deliberation over density and housing type
• Infrastructure conditions constrained: ministerial approval pathways limit the infrastructure improvements and impact conditions cities may require, shifting capacity costs to existing residents
Recommended: Oppose — bill strips cities of zoning authority, eliminates public review, and mandates approvals without preserving local infrastructure protection tools; fetch full text recommended to confirm scope of §65912.156 amendment.

analysis.notes: AB 1710 amends Section 65589.5 (the Housing Accountability Act) and adds Section 65958, which together appear to further restrict cities' ability to deny or condition housing projects and likely expand ministerial/by-right approval pathways, directly preempting local zoning discretion and reducing public review. The bill's unanimous committee passage and dual referral to Housing & Community Development and Local Government committees signals substantial local preemption provisions, and the HAA amendment mechanism typically strips cities of the ability to apply local standards that aren't state-defined "objective" criteria, while the new Section 65958 likely adds another streamlining or approval mandate layer.
analysis.comms_brief:
AB 1710 amends the Housing Accountability Act (Gov. Code §65589.5) and adds a new §65958 to further restrict cities' authority to deny, condition, or apply local standards to housing projects, likely extending ministerial or streamlined approval mandates.
• HAA amendments typically prohibit cities from applying subjective or locally-adopted standards, forcing approval of projects that conflict with community character goals
• New Section 65958 likely creates an additional by-right or streamlined approval pathway, removing discretionary review, public hearings, or design review for qualifying projects
• Expanded HAA liability exposure increases legal risk for cities that attempt to apply local conditions, effectively chilling legitimate local planning decisions
• Unfunded compliance obligations — tracking HAA findings, defending approval denials — shift administrative and legal costs onto city budgets without state offset
Recommended: Oppose — HAA amendments historically strip cities of meaningful review authority, and the addition of §65958 suggests a new mandate layer with no local mitigation mechanism; full bill text should be reviewed to confirm scope.

analysis.comms_brief:
AB 2074 creates a state-mandated ministerial approval pathway for housing developments in regional transit hub districts, adding Government Code §65913.13 to explicitly override local zoning, discretionary review, and site-specific planning authority in designated downtown transit zones.
• Explicit state preemption of local zoning: cities in qualifying transit hub districts lose authority to apply locally adopted land use standards to covered projects
• Ministerial by-right approval eliminates CEQA review, design review, public hearings, and conditional use permits for qualifying downtown housing developments
• Mandatory approval of qualifying projects forces cities to permit density and development volumes beyond what local planning processes would choose
• Appropriation and state-directed program structure creates unfunded compliance burdens and limits cities' ability to require developer-funded infrastructure improvements commensurate with project impacts
Recommended: Oppose — the bill strips cities of all meaningful discretion over high-impact downtown housing projects without providing any mechanism to ensure infrastructure capacity, maintain design standards, or hold developers accountable to community-adopted plans

analysis.comms_brief:
SB 908 adds Government Code §65850.73 to limit local governments' authority to block or condition residential window retrofits that meet California Energy Code standards, effectively preempting local design and permitting discretion over qualifying projects.
• Adds a Government Code land use section that could prohibit cities from applying local design review, historic district standards, or objective design criteria to window replacement projects
• Removes or limits discretionary review authority over retrofit projects by mandating compliance with state energy code as a sufficient approval basis, bypassing local conditions
• Creates a state-preemption framework that, depending on final bill language, could be expanded by courts or future legislation to cover a broader class of building modifications
Recommended: Monitor — bill text is still in amendment and full operative language is not yet available; local control risk is real but scope depends heavily on final statutory language in §65850.73 and §4754.
